result:
Somewhere, a tide is turning. Not the great, heaving turn of oceanic tides, but a smaller, more personal one. A tide of light in a room where someone, just now, decided to open a curtain they’ve left closed for weeks. The dust motes, startled, dance in the sudden sun. That’s where we begin.

We carry so many silent inventories. The precise heft of a favourite mug. The exact timbre of a laugh from a voice we haven’t heard in years, preserved like a fossil in amber somewhere behind the sternum. The specific, damp-dog smell of a childhood raincoat. We are librarians of the intimate, cataloguing sensations we never knew we’d need to recall until the world goes grey and we desperately thumb through the files.

Right now, as you read this, your body is a symphony of minor miracles you’ve politely ignored. Your heart, that tireless drummer, has kept a rhythm since before you were aware of having a self to be aware of. Your lungs, twin bellows, are drawing in atoms that once swirled in the breath of Caesar, of Cleopatra, of a forgotten baker in medieval France. You are, literally, stardust that has learned to ponder its own origins. Isn’t that absurd? Isn’t that magnificent?

We build our lives on routines, on the comforting railway tracks of habit. Coffee at seven. The same route to work. The same chair. And yet, the universe is constantly whispering of chaos, of wildness, just beyond the fence. A dandelion cracks through pristine asphalt. A sparrow, with a brain the size of a pea, navigates by the magnetism of the planet itself. We tidy our gardens while the cosmos is busy flinging comets and birthing novas in spectacular, wasteful, glorious explosions. There’s a tension there, between our need for order and the thrilling, terrifying disorder of simply *being*.

So much of our energy is spent building walls. Walls of opinion, of identity, of carefully curated online personas. We defend these little forts fiercely. But what if, just for a moment, we laid down the bricks? What if we admitted that we, too, are sometimes the villain in someone else’s story? That our convictions might have soft, unfocused edges? That it’s okay to say, “I don’t know,” and truly mean it? There is a profound relief in unclenching the mind.

Think of hands. Such ordinary things. But a hand can cradle a newborn’s head with a tenderness that defies physics. That same hand can shape clay, stitch a wound, plant a seed that becomes an oak tree that will outlive generations of hands. It can make a fist, yes, but it can also open, palm up, in a gesture of offering or surrender. Most of the history of human kindness and human cruelty is written in the language of hands.

We are haunted by time. Not by ghosts, but by the phantom limbs of our past selves and the shadowy outlines of our future ones. We mourn versions of ourselves we can never be again—the fearless child, the passionate young adult—even as we fear the versions we have yet to become. We are always in the process of disappearance and arrival. The person who started reading this essay is not quite the same as the person finishing it. A few neurons have fired; a memory has subtly shifted; a minute has passed, gone forever.

Yet, in this river of constant change, we seek anchors. Often, they are other people. To love someone is to appoint them a custodian of a piece of your soul. It is a terrifying act of faith. It is saying, “Here, hold this. I trust you not to drop it.” And in their eyes, we sometimes catch a glimpse of our own reflection, not as we are, but as we *could be*—softer, braver, more understood.

There is a quiet heroism in the ordinary that goes utterly uncelebrated. The person who gets out of bed despite the leaden weight of despair. The one who chooses kindness when bitterness would be easier. The person who hears a strange sound in the night and still gets up to check, not out of bravery, but out of a deep, stubborn commitment to the well-being of the house and everyone in it. We are surrounded by these tiny, daily acts of courage, ours and others’, and we often forget to salute them.

Maybe the point isn’t to find some grand, universal meaning etched in the stars. Maybe the meaning is in the etching itself. In the care you take making a meal. In the attention you pay to a friend’s story. In the willingness to sit with your own silence, without rushing to fill it with noise. The universe may be vast and indifferent, but in our little corner of it, we can generate warmth. We can create pockets of meaning, like lighting a candle in a vast, dark cathedral. The darkness doesn’t care, but the candle matters immensely to those who see it, and feel its heat.

So, as this tide of words begins to recede, leaving its scattered shells of thought on the shore of your mind, consider this: You are here. Now. The curtain is open. The light is on the dust, making it magic. Your heart is drumming. Your lungs are drawing in ancient, shared air. The inventory of your life is yours to take, to add to, to cherish.

The world outside is wild and beautiful and broken. Your hands are capable of both fists and cradles. Choose the cradle more often. Build fewer walls. Tend your tiny, defiant candle. And remember, amidst the chaos and the quiet, the most revolutionary act might simply be to pay attention. To this breath. To this moment. To the incredible, ordinary, impossible fact of being alive, together, on this spinning blue rock, under the same endless, star-dusted sky.

The tide has turned. What will you do with your wet feet?
